variables.content: The effective mass m* of conduction electrons in two-dimensional electron systems formed at GaAs/AlGaAs heterojunctions was measured as a function of carrier density over the interaction-parameter range 0.8 ≤ r_{s} ≤ 6, where r_{s} is the usual two-dimensional dimensionless interaction parameter equal to the ratio of the average Coulomb energy to the Fermi energy. The principal experimental findings are: (a) toward low carrier density (large r_{s}) the measured m* increases strongly, reaching an enhancement of approximately 40% above the single-particle GaAs conduction-band effective mass at r_{s} ≈ 5; and (b) in the higher-density regime 0.8 ≤ r_{s} ≤ 2 the measured m* lies about 10% below the single-particle GaAs conduction-band effective mass after applying a correction that accounts for the known non-parabolicity of the GaAs conduction band. In this statement "single-particle GaAs conduction-band effective mass" denotes the effective mass obtained from GaAs band-structure (non-interacting) considerations evaluated at the Fermi energy.

Using the same vertical tunnelling spectrometer and measuring tunnelling from the laterally wide parasitic injection region (which behaves as an effectively two-dimensional electron gas), the interaction-renormalised effective mass of electrons in the two-dimensional parasitic regions is
      $$
      m_{2\mathrm{D}}^{*}=(0.062\pm0.002)m_{e},
      $$
      where $m_{2\mathrm{D}}^{*}$ denotes the 2D effective mass; this value is consistent with independent determinations in the literature and approaches the nominal bulk GaAs low-density mass $m_{3\mathrm{D}}^{*}=0.067m_{e}$ after modest interaction-induced renormalisation is accounted for.

For a GaAs inversion-layer geometry and impurity-parameter set identical to those used for the hole calculations but using the GaAs conduction-band effective mass (which is substantially smaller than the hole effective mass), numerical resistivity calculations predict essentially negligible temperature dependence of $\rho(T)$ at low temperatures for 2D electron densities down to $n=0.38\times10^{10}\,\mathrm{cm^{-2}}$; this weak temperature dependence arises because the two-dimensional density of states per spin $g(E_F)=m^*/(2\pi\hbar^2)$ is small for the low effective mass, leading to weak finite-temperature screening and therefore a suppressed temperature-dependent impurity-scattering contribution compared with the heavier-mass holes.

For a two‑dimensional electron gas confined to a single GaAs quantum well with effective mass $m^{*}$ and with Fermi energy $E_{f}$ measured from the bottom of the conduction subband, the areal electron density $n_{2D}$ is given by the zero‑temperature relation
      $n_{2D} = \dfrac{4\pi m^{*}}{h^{2}}\,E_{f}$,
      where $h$ is Planck's constant; this relation is applicable as a design conversion between $E_{f}$ and $n_{2D}$ for estimating sheet densities in the low‑temperature ($T\lesssim20\ \mathrm{K}$) devices considered.

For conduction electrons in GaAs, the energy-dependent effective mass increases with carrier kinetic energy due to conduction-band nonparabolicity, so that for two-dimensional electron densities $n$ a higher $n$ (larger Fermi energy and larger average in-plane kinetic energy) produces a larger nonparabolicity-induced mass enhancement; consequently, observing an $\sim11\%$ enhancement at $n\simeq1.2\times10^{12}\ \mathrm{cm}^{-2}$ and a smaller $\sim7\%$ enhancement at $n=8.7\times10^{11}\ \mathrm{cm}^{-2}$ is consistent with a conduction-band nonparabolicity explanation for the measured $m^*/m_b^*$ ratio.

Far-infrared cyclotron-resonance measurements performed on the illuminated and non-illuminated GaAs–Al_xGa_{1-x}As interface samples with two-dimensional electron-gas sheet densities in the range 1.1×10^{12} to 1.6×10^{12} cm^{-2} yield an electron cyclotron effective mass m^{*} ≈ 1.12\,m_{\mathrm{bulk}}^{*}, where m_{\mathrm{bulk}}^{*} denotes the conduction-band effective mass of bulk GaAs; this measured mass enhancement at Fermi energies of order several tens of meV above the band edge is attributed primarily to conduction-band nonparabolicity.

Measured peak absorption quantum efficiency for the strained InP/In$_{0.48}$Ga$_{0.52}$As QWIP pixels is $\eta_{p}\approx12\%$, while measured peak absorption quantum efficiency for the comparison AlGaAs/GaAs focal-plane-array pixels is $\eta_{p}\approx4.9\%$, giving an observed ratio $\eta_{\mathrm{InP}}/\eta_{\mathrm{GaAs}}\approx2.5$; accounting for the measured difference in two-dimensional electron density $n_{2D}$ (the GaAs device has $n_{2D}$ smaller by a factor of $\sim1.7$), this enhancement in $\eta$ is consistent with the smaller electron effective mass in the strained In$_{x}$Ga$_{1-x}$As wells ($m^{*}_{\mathrm{InGaAs}}<m^{*}_{\mathrm{GaAs}}$) and is numerically comparable to the effective-mass ratio $m^{*}_{\mathrm{GaAs}}/m^{*}_{\mathrm{InGaAs}}\approx1.6$ reported for $x\sim0.5$.

Conversion of reported in-plane fields for full spin polarization of high-mobility GaAs two-dimensional hole systems into effective many-body $g^{*}$ values using the same polarization-field relation $B_{P}=(h^{2}/2\pi\mu_{B})(n/(m^{*}g^{*}))$ (with $n$ the hole areal density and an appropriate hole cyclotron effective mass $m^{*}$ for the hole band at the relevant density) yields a qualitative dependence of $g^{*}$ on $r_{s}$ that matches the decreasing-enhancement trend observed here for GaAs electrons; this similarity between low-disorder GaAs 2D electrons and holes suggests that the origin of the decreasing $g^{*}$ enhancement with increasing $r_{s}$ may be intrinsic to GaAs systems (for example tied to finite-thickness effects or spin-orbit physics) rather than an idiosyncrasy of a particular electron sample, while noting that hole band-structure specifics such as strong spin-orbit coupling and nonparabolicity can modify $m^{*}$ and $g_{b}$ relative to the electron case.

For the GaAs-based two-dimensional electron systems used in these experiments the electron effective mass entering eigenenergy and density-of-states formulas is taken as the standard bulk GaAs conduction-band value $m^{*}=0.067\,m_{0}$ (with $m_{0}$ the free-electron mass); the proposition is that using this constant value for $m^{*}$ is accurate for the heterostructure and density regime examined and that variations of $m^{*}$ due to band nonparabolicity or confinement are negligible for the analyses performed.

For a two-dimensional, noninteracting electron gas with effective mass m^* and carrier density N the zero-temperature Fermi energy is E_F = (pi ħ^2 N) / m^*, so a small density change delta N produces a chemical-potential change delta mu_F = (pi ħ^2 / m^*) delta N. Using the GaAs effective electron mass m^* = 0.067 m_e (with m_e the free-electron rest mass) and substituting delta N = 0.7 × 10^14 m^{-2} gives delta mu_F ≈ 0.22 meV; the proposition is that this 2D free-electron relation with m^* = 0.067 m_e is applicable as an estimate for the high-density quantum-well HM2DEG in the studied parameter range.
      Standard 2D electron gas relations; Jenichen et al., Section III.B
